title: Can Glasses Fix Astigmatism? What Your Prescription Actually Does
description: Glasses don't cure astigmatism, but they correct it effectively. Learn how cylindrical lenses work, what your CYL and AXIS numbers mean, and when to see an optometrist.
tldr: Glasses correct astigmatism but don't cure it. Your prescription includes CYL (cylinder) and AXIS values that compensate for the irregular shape of your cornea. Most people with astigmatism see perfectly well with properly prescribed glasses. The correction works immediately — no adjustment period for mild to moderate astigmatism.

tldrStats.label: Canadians have some degree of astigmatism

definition.text: A common refractive error caused by an irregularly shaped cornea or lens. Instead of being round like a basketball, the eye's surface is shaped more like a football, causing light to focus at multiple points rather than one. This creates blurry or distorted vision at all distances.

sections.content:
Glasses don't fix or cure astigmatism — they compensate for it. Your cornea's irregular shape bends light unevenly, so your lenses bend it back into proper focus. The result is clear vision while you're wearing them.

The correction uses a cylindrical lens component. Unlike the spherical (SPH) part of your prescription that corrects nearsightedness or farsightedness uniformly, the cylinder (CYL) corrects in a specific direction defined by the AXIS value.

Think of it this way: if your cornea curves more steeply in one direction, the lens adds extra correction in that same direction to even things out. The axis tells your optician exactly what angle to orient that correction.

Most people with mild to moderate astigmatism see 20/20 with properly prescribed glasses. There's usually no adaptation period — you put them on and things look sharper right away.

sections.h2: Understanding CYL and AXIS on Your Prescription
sections.content:
If you have astigmatism, your prescription will have three key numbers for each eye:

  • SPH (Sphere): Corrects nearsightedness (-) or farsightedness (+). You might have this even without astigmatism.
  • CYL (Cylinder): The amount of astigmatism correction needed. Ranges from -0.25 to -6.00 or more. Higher numbers mean more astigmatism.
  • AXIS: A number between 1 and 180 that indicates the angle of your astigmatism. This tells the lab exactly how to orient the cylindrical correction in the lens.

A prescription reading OD: -2.00 / -1.25 x 090 means: right eye is -2.00 nearsighted, with -1.25 of astigmatism at the 90-degree axis. An eye exam determines these numbers precisely.

If your CYL value is -0.25 to -0.75, your astigmatism is mild. Many people with this level don't even notice it without correction. Values above -1.50 typically cause noticeable blur that glasses significantly improve.

sections.h2: Glasses vs. Contacts vs. Surgery for Astigmatism
sections.content:
OptionHow It WorksBest ForLimitations
GlassesCylindrical lens correctionAll astigmatism levels, easiest optionMust be worn to see clearly
Toric contact lensesWeighted contacts that stay oriented correctlyActive people, cosmetic preferenceMore expensive, requires fitting, can rotate
LASIK / PRKReshapes cornea with laserCandidates wanting permanent correctionNot reversible, not suitable for everyone

For most people, glasses are the simplest and most cost-effective way to manage astigmatism. They work immediately, need no maintenance beyond cleaning, and your prescription can be updated as your eyes change. Toric contacts work well too but cost more and require more care. Surgery is a permanent option for eligible candidates who want freedom from corrective lenses.

When comparing options, consider your daily routine and visual priorities rather than just price. The "best" choice is the one that matches how you actually use your eyes throughout the day. Someone who works on a computer for 8 hours has very different needs than someone who primarily drives or reads. An honest conversation with your optometrist about your daily visual demands will point you toward the right option far more effectively than online reviews.

Keep in mind that you're not locked into one choice forever. Many people own multiple pairs for different situations — dedicated computer glasses for the office, progressives for daily wear, and prescription sunglasses for driving. Charm Optical carries 500+ frames across every style and price point, making it easy to build a collection that covers all your visual needs. Their opticians can advise which lens types pair best with each frame style. Same-day glasses are available for many single vision prescriptions.

sections.h2: Can Astigmatism Get Worse Over Time?
sections.content:
Astigmatism can change gradually over the years, though dramatic shifts are uncommon in adults. A few things worth knowing:

  • Children: Astigmatism often stabilizes by the late teens or early twenties. Regular eye exams for kids catch changes early.
  • Adults: Small shifts of 0.25-0.50 over several years are normal. Your prescription update handles this.
  • Wearing glasses doesn't make it worse. This is a persistent myth. Glasses correct astigmatism — they don't change the shape of your cornea.
  • Not wearing glasses doesn't make it worse either. You'll just see less clearly without them.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every one to two years for adults. If you notice increasing blur, headaches, or eye strain — especially at night — your astigmatism may have changed enough to warrant an updated prescription.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Astigmatism doesn't always cause obvious symptoms, especially when mild. But common signs include:

  • Blurry vision at all distances (not just far or near)
  • Difficulty seeing at night, especially halos around lights while driving
  • Squinting to see clearly
  • Headaches after prolonged reading or screen time
  • Eye strain or fatigue, especially toward the end of the day

Many people don't realize they have astigmatism until their first eye exam. If you haven't had your eyes checked recently, it's worth booking one — particularly if any of these symptoms sound familiar. Insurance plans in Alberta typically cover the cost of your exam.

If you're experiencing any of these signs regularly, it's worth getting a professional assessment rather than guessing. Many vision problems develop gradually — your brain compensates for declining vision so effectively that you may not notice the change until it's significant. A comprehensive eye exam can detect issues that self-assessment misses, including early signs of conditions like glaucoma and macular degeneration that have no symptoms in their early stages.

The Canadian Association of Optometrists recommends eye exams every 1-2 years for adults. Children should be examined annually since vision problems can significantly impact learning and development. In Alberta, children under 18 receive coverage through Alberta Health. Adults with diabetes or a family history of eye disease should also get annual exams. Book an appointment at Charm Optical — they accept most Alberta insurance providers through direct billing.

Buying glasses online might save money upfront, but the fit and vision quality often suffer without professional measurements. Your pupillary distance (PD), segment height for progressive lenses, and frame alignment all need to be measured precisely. Even a 2mm error in PD can cause eye strain, headaches, and blurry vision — especially with progressive or high-prescription lenses.

An experienced optician also considers factors that online tools can't measure: how the frame sits on your nose bridge, whether the temples create pressure behind your ears, and how the lens angle affects your vision. These adjustments are the difference between glasses you love wearing and glasses that sit in a drawer.

At Charm Optical in Edmonton, every pair of glasses includes a professional fitting at no extra charge. faqs.a: Glasses correct astigmatism so you see clearly while wearing them, but they don't permanently fix or cure it. Your cornea shape stays the same — the lenses compensate for the irregular curvature. Only laser surgery (LASIK/PRK) can permanently reshape the cornea.

faqs.q: Do I need special glasses for astigmatism?
faqs.a: Not really. Any prescription glasses can include astigmatism correction. Your prescription simply has additional CYL and AXIS values that tell the lab how to grind the cylindrical component into your lenses. No special frames are needed.

Astigmatism is extremely common and not a disease. About one in three Canadians has some degree of astigmatism. It's a refractive error — a variation in eye shape — that's easily corrected with glasses, contacts, or surgery.

faqs.q: Can astigmatism go away on its own?
faqs.a: In rare cases, very mild astigmatism in young children can decrease as the eye develops. In adults, astigmatism doesn't go away on its own, though it may change slightly over the years.

faqs.q: Where can I get glasses in Edmonton?
faqs.a: Charm Optical at 5035 Ellerslie Rd SW in South Edmonton carries 500+ designer frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, and Tom Ford. They offer same-day glasses, professional fitting, and direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers. Book online at see.charmoptical.ca.

faqs.q: How much do glasses cost in Canada?
faqs.a: Glasses in Canada cost $150-$800+ depending on frame brand and lens type. Budget frames with single vision: $150-$250. Designer frames with progressive lenses: $400-$800+. Most employer insurance plans cover $200-$500. Charm Optical offers competitive pricing and direct-bills insurance.
